mainEntity.name: Why does my child's relationship with food textures feels like walking through a minefield. what seems like a normal bite to me can trigger gagging, tears, or outright refusal from them. it started when we introduced baby foods — they would only accept perfectly smooth purees. when i tried to add texture, even tiny soft pieces, they would gag and spit everything out. i thought they'd grow out of it, but they haven't. now they're older and their 'safe' food list is shrinking, not growing. they categorize foods by texture more than taste. smooth is usually safe: yogurt, pudding, mashed potatoes (but only if there are absolutely no lumps), applesauce, certain smooth soups. crunchy can work if it's predictable: specific crackers, certain chips, dry cereal. but anything in between — anything with mixed textures, soft lumps, 'bits,' or unexpected pieces — is rejected instantly. a sandwich is too many textures at once. fruit with skin is impossible. rice makes them gag. meat is extremely difficult. sometimes they gag just looking at food. they seem to know what the texture will be before it touches their mouth. if they accidentally get a texture they didn't expect, it can ruin the whole meal — sometimes they won't eat anything else. i've tried everything i can think of. hiding vegetables doesn't work — they detect the texture change. encouraging 'just one bite' leads to gagging and more aversion. rewards don't help when the food genuinely makes them feel sick. i'm running out of ideas and their nutrition is suffering. they're embarrassed at birthday parties when they can't eat the cake. family meals are stressful. i don't know if this is sensory processing, anxiety, oral motor issues, or something else entirely. i need to understand what's happening and what tools can actually help them tolerate — and eventually maybe even enjoy — more textures?

acceptedAnswer.text: ORAL SENSORY PROCESSING DIFFERENCES AFFECTING FEEDING / TEXTURE-BASED FOOD AVERSION / SENSORY FOOD SELECTIVITY / ARFID (AVOIDANT/RESTRICTIVE FOOD INTAKE DISORDER) WITH SENSORY COMPONENT / ORAL HYPERSENSITIVITY! Food texture aversions occur when the oral sensory system processes certain textures as aversive, triggering protective responses like gagging, spitting, or refusal. This is a neurological response, not willful behavior — the brain interprets the sensory input from certain textures as threatening or overwhelming. Oral sensory processing involves detecting and interpreting information from food in the mouth: texture, temperature, taste, and movement. Children with oral sensory hypersensitivity (over-responsivity) experience ordinary textures as intensely unpleasant or even painful. The gag reflex may be hypersensitive, triggering at textures that wouldn't cause gagging in typical development. Texture aversions commonly affect mixed textures (foods with multiple consistencies), lumpy or chunky foods, chewy foods, and foods with unexpected pieces. Without intervention, the range of accepted foods often narrows further. Therapy involves systematic desensitization, oral motor development, and gradual texture progression — moving at the child's pace while building tolerance.

mainEntity.name: What is Oral Sensory Processing Differences Affecting Feeding / Texture-Based Food Aversion / Sensory Food Selectivity / ARFID (Avoidant/Restrictive Food Intake Disorder) with Sensory Component / Oral Hypersensitivity?

acceptedAnswer.text: Food texture aversions occur when the oral sensory system processes certain textures as aversive, triggering protective responses like gagging, spitting, or refusal. This is a neurological response, not willful behavior — the brain interprets the sensory input from certain textures as threatening or overwhelming. Oral sensory processing involves detecting and interpreting information from food in the mouth: texture, temperature, taste, and movement. Children with oral sensory hypersensitivity (over-responsivity) experience ordinary textures as intensely unpleasant or even painful. The gag reflex may be hypersensitive, triggering at textures that wouldn't cause gagging in typical development. Texture aversions commonly affect mixed textures (foods with multiple consistencies), lumpy or chunky foods, chewy foods, and foods with unexpected pieces. Without intervention, the range of accepted foods often narrows further. Therapy involves systematic desensitization, oral motor development, and gradual texture progression — moving at the child's pace while building tolerance. Common signs include: Gagging on specific food textures (lumps, chunks, chewy, mixed), Strong preference for smooth, pureed, or uniform textures, Refusal to try new textures even if taste is liked, Gagging or refusal before food touches mouth (visual aversion), Extreme reaction to unexpected texture (piece in smooth food).

mainEntity.name: How does a Oral Sensory Exploration Tools (Textured Chewies) help with oral sensory processing differences affecting feeding?

acceptedAnswer.text: Before addressing food textures directly, children often benefit from non-food oral sensory input that prepares the mouth for varied sensations. Textured chewies — safe, chewable tools with different surface textures (bumpy, ridged, smooth, nubbed) — provide controlled oral sensory input without the pressure of eating. The child can explore textures in their mouth at their own pace, building tolerance and awareness. Chewing also provides proprioceptive input that can be organizing and calming. These tools help normalize the experience of different textures in the mouth before food textures are introduced. They're used in oral sensory warm-up routines before meals or throughout the day to build general oral sensory tolerance. Price: ₹300–1,200. DIY option: Select food-grade silicone textured chewies from therapeutic suppliers (ARK Therapeutic, Chewigem, etc.). Start with texture child tolerates, then gradually introduce different textures. Use before meals as 'oral warm-up.' Can also use during the day for sensory regulation. Let child control the tool — never force. Some children prefer vibrating oral tools (adds additional sensory input). Ensure tools are appropriate size for child's age and mouth. Replace when worn. Safety: Use only food-grade, BPA-free materials designed for oral use. Check for wear and replace damaged tools. Supervise use to prevent choking.

mainEntity.name: How does a Vibrating Oral Motor Tool help with oral sensory processing differences affecting feeding?

acceptedAnswer.text: Vibration provides intense but non-threatening sensory input to the mouth and face. For children with oral hypersensitivity, vibration can help 'wake up' the oral sensory system in a controlled way, reducing over-responsivity. The vibration provides deep input that can help normalize oral sensory processing. Vibrating tools used on lips, cheeks, gums, and tongue can reduce hypersensitivity and prepare the mouth for varied food textures. The predictable nature of vibration (consistent, controlled by the child or therapist) makes it less threatening than unpredictable food textures. Used before meals or during oral motor therapy, vibration can improve tolerance for subsequent texture experiences. Price: ₹500–2,000. DIY option: Select vibrating oral tools designed for therapeutic use (Z-Vibe by ARK Therapeutic is a common choice). Start with vibration on less sensitive areas (cheeks, lips) before moving toward more sensitive areas (gums, tongue). Let child control or indicate readiness. Use before meals as part of oral warm-up routine. Can use different attachments for varied input. Short sessions (1-3 minutes) before progressing to eating. Some children find vibration immediately organizing; others need gradual introduction. Safety: Use only therapeutic-grade tools designed for oral use. Never force. Start with low intensity and build tolerance. Stop if child shows distress.

mainEntity.name: How does a Texture Progression Food Kit (Flavor Sprays and Dips) help with oral sensory processing differences affecting feeding?

acceptedAnswer.text: Texture aversion is primarily about the physical sensation, not taste. This creates an opportunity: children can learn to associate preferred flavors with progressively more challenging textures. Flavor sprays and dips allow the child to add their preferred taste to new texture experiences. If a child likes a certain flavor (butter, cheese, ranch, chocolate, etc.), that flavor can be applied to a texture they're working on tolerating. The familiar, liked taste provides positive association while the new texture is introduced. This 'flavor bridge' technique helps the brain create positive associations with textures that were previously rejected. It works with the child's preferences rather than against them. Price: ₹200–800. DIY option: Identify 2-3 flavors the child genuinely enjoys (butter, cheese, ranch, ketchup, chocolate, cinnamon sugar, etc.). Keep these available at meals. When introducing a texture challenge, let the child apply their preferred flavor. The flavor doesn't need to 'make sense' — if they want ranch on apple, that's fine for this purpose. The goal is positive texture exposure, not conventional eating. Gradually reduce the amount of flavor as texture tolerance builds. Some children also benefit from flavor sprays that add familiar taste without changing texture significantly. Safety: Be aware of food allergies. Use age-appropriate foods. Focus on positive exposure, not forcing consumption.

mainEntity.name: How does a Graded Texture Food Samples (Therapy Progression) help with oral sensory processing differences affecting feeding?

acceptedAnswer.text: Texture tolerance builds through systematic progression — not by forcing the most challenging textures, but by moving through carefully graded steps. A texture progression typically moves from: smooth purees → slightly textured purees (tiny soft bits) → more textured (visible pieces) → soft mashables → soft solids → harder textures → mixed textures. Having actual food samples at each level allows visual and tactile exploration before eating. The child can see and touch the texture they'll work on. This reduces anxiety and surprise. Commercially available or home-prepared graded texture samples become the therapy 'ladder' — each step slightly more challenging than the last, with the child moving up only when ready. Price: ₹0–500 (mostly food costs). DIY option: Create a texture progression for a specific food the child is working on. Example with banana: smooth puree → puree with tiny soft pieces → mashed with visible chunks → soft banana pieces → whole banana slices. Create visual 'ladder' showing steps. Let child see and touch each level. Work on tolerance at current level before moving up. Other examples: yogurt (smooth → with small fruit pieces → with larger pieces → with crunchy toppings); pasta (very soft, overcooked → normal → with small sauce pieces → with chunky sauce). Document what level child is at for each food category. Safety: Respect choking hazard guidelines for child's age. Monitor for actual difficulty chewing/swallowing vs. sensory aversion. Work with feeding therapist for significant difficulties.

mainEntity.name: How does a Food Interaction Tools (Picks, Tweezers, Mashers) help with oral sensory processing differences affecting feeding?

acceptedAnswer.text: Direct hand contact with challenging textures can trigger aversion before food even reaches the mouth. Food interaction tools create distance between the child and the texture while still allowing exploration. Food picks and toothpicks let children poke, explore, and eventually spear foods without full hand contact. Tweezers and tongs allow picking up and moving foods. Child-sized mashers let them create textures themselves (mashing = control). This indirect interaction reduces sensory overwhelm while keeping the child engaged with food. It also gives them control — they decide when and how to interact. Control reduces anxiety and increases willingness to explore. Price: ₹100–500. DIY option: Provide food picks, toothpicks, small tongs or tweezers, child-sized forks, and mashers at meals. Let child use tools to interact with foods they're not yet eating. Encourage exploration: 'Can you poke it? What does it feel like? Can you squish it?' The progression: look at food → touch with tool → touch with finger → touch to lips → tiny taste → small bite. Tools help with early steps. Some children enjoy mashing or cutting foods themselves — this gives control over texture. Don't require eating; just interaction. Safety: Use age-appropriate tools without sharp points. Supervise use. Focus on exploration, not forcing eating.

mainEntity.name: How does a Sensory Play Materials for Non-Food Texture Exposure help with oral sensory processing differences affecting feeding?

acceptedAnswer.text: Tactile sensitivity often extends beyond food to general touch. Children who are tactilely defensive may benefit from broader sensory play that builds overall texture tolerance. When the hands become more comfortable with varied textures through play, the mouth often follows. Sensory play materials — play dough, kinetic sand, water beads, slime, sensory bins with varied textures — build general tactile tolerance. The play context removes pressure and makes texture exploration fun. While not directly about food, this 'texture play' helps the nervous system become more comfortable with varied sensory input overall, which transfers to oral texture tolerance. Price: ₹200–1,000. DIY option: Provide regular opportunities for sensory play with varied textures. Options: play dough (smooth, then add texture with beads or rice); kinetic sand; cooked pasta for squishing; water beads; slime (varying thickness); sensory bins with dry materials (rice, beans, sand). Let child control their level of engagement — some children need to start with tools before using hands. Gradually increase texture variety and 'messy' quality. Connect to food when appropriate: 'This feels squishy like mashed potatoes.' Regular sensory play reduces overall tactile defensiveness. Safety: Supervise for mouthing of non-food items, especially with young children. Use non-toxic materials. Be aware of choking hazards with small items.

@graph.@id: https://pinnacleblooms.org/intervention-techniques/feeding / sensory processing / oral motor / pediatric occupational therapy / speech-language pathology/food texture sensitivity / oral sensory processing / texture progression / picky eating / arfid / sensory-based feeding difficulties/9-materials-that-help-with-food-texture-aversions#howto
@graph.name: How to Help a Child with Oral Sensory Processing Differences Affecting Feeding
@graph.description: A graded approach using evidence-based materials. Food texture aversions are rooted in oral sensory processing differences — the nervous system interprets certain textures as aversive or threatening. This triggers protective responses (gagging, refusal, spitting) that are involuntary, not willful. Intervention addresses both the sensory processing component (building tolerance to oral sensory input) and the motor component (developing oral motor skills to efficiently manage textures). The approach is systematic, gradual, and positive. Forcing or pressuring increases aversion. Success comes from understanding the child's specific sensory profile, starting from their current accepted foods/textures, and progressing through carefully graded steps at their pace.

supply.name: Texture Progression Food Kit (Flavor Sprays and Dips)

Texture aversions commonly affect mixed textures (foods with multiple consistencies), lumpy or chunky foods, chewy foods, and foods with unexpected pieces. Without intervention, the range of accepted foods often narrows further. Therapy involves systematic desensitization, oral motor development, and gradual texture progression — moving at the child's pace while building tolerance. signOrSymptom.name: Gagging on specific food textures (lumps, chunks, chewy, mixed)
Strong preference for smooth, pureed, or uniform textures
Refusal to try new textures even if taste is liked
Gagging or refusal before food touches mouth (visual aversion)
Extreme reaction to unexpected texture (piece in smooth food)
Spitting out foods with 'bits' or mixed textures
Difficulty with age-appropriate textures (still on purees past infancy)
Limited food variety based on texture categories

item.name: Oral Sensory Exploration Tools (Textured Chewies)
item.description: Before addressing food textures directly, children often benefit from non-food oral sensory input that prepares the mouth for varied sensations. Textured chewies — safe, chewable tools with different surface textures (bumpy, ridged, smooth, nubbed) — provide controlled oral sensory input without the pressure of eating. The child can explore textures in their mouth at their own pace, building tolerance and awareness. Chewing also provides proprioceptive input that can be organizing and calming. These tools help normalize the experience of different textures in the mouth before food textures are introduced. They're used in oral sensory warm-up routines before meals or throughout the day to build general oral sensory tolerance.

additionalProperty.value: Select food-grade silicone textured chewies from therapeutic suppliers (ARK Therapeutic, Chewigem, etc.). Start with texture child tolerates, then gradually introduce different textures. Use before meals as 'oral warm-up.' Can also use during the day for sensory regulation. Let child control the tool — never force. Some children prefer vibrating oral tools (adds additional sensory input). Ensure tools are appropriate size for child's age and mouth. Replace when worn.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Use only food-grade, BPA-free materials designed for oral use. Check for wear and replace damaged tools. Supervise use to prevent choking.

item.description: Vibration provides intense but non-threatening sensory input to the mouth and face. The vibration provides deep input that can help normalize oral sensory processing. Vibrating tools used on lips, cheeks, gums, and tongue can reduce hypersensitivity and prepare the mouth for varied food textures. The predictable nature of vibration (consistent, controlled by the child or therapist) makes it less threatening than unpredictable food textures. Used before meals or during oral motor therapy, vibration can improve tolerance for subsequent texture experiences.

Use before meals as part of oral warm-up routine. Can use different attachments for varied input. Short sessions (1-3 minutes) before progressing to eating. Some children find vibration immediately organizing; others need gradual introduction.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Use only therapeutic-grade tools designed for oral use. Never force. Start with low intensity and build tolerance. Stop if child shows distress.

item.name: Texture Progression Food Kit (Flavor Sprays and Dips)
item.description: Texture aversion is primarily about the physical sensation, not taste. This creates an opportunity: children can learn to associate preferred flavors with progressively more challenging textures. Flavor sprays and dips allow the child to add their preferred taste to new texture experiences. If a child likes a certain flavor (butter, cheese, ranch, chocolate, etc.), that flavor can be applied to a texture they're working on tolerating. The familiar, liked taste provides positive association while the new texture is introduced. This 'flavor bridge' technique helps the brain create positive associations with textures that were previously rejected. It works with the child's preferences rather than against them.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Identify 2-3 flavors the child genuinely enjoys (butter, cheese, ranch, ketchup, chocolate, cinnamon sugar, etc.). Keep these available at meals. When introducing a texture challenge, let the child apply their preferred flavor. The flavor doesn't need to 'make sense' — if they want ranch on apple, that's fine for this purpose. The goal is positive texture exposure, not conventional eating. Gradually reduce the amount of flavor as texture tolerance builds. Some children also benefit from flavor sprays that add familiar taste without changing texture significantly.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Be aware of food allergies. Use age-appropriate foods. Focus on positive exposure, not forcing consumption.

item.name: Graded Texture Food Samples (Therapy Progression)
item.description: Texture tolerance builds through systematic progression — not by forcing the most challenging textures, but by moving through carefully graded steps. A texture progression typically moves from: smooth purees → slightly textured purees (tiny soft bits) → more textured (visible pieces) → soft mashables → soft solids → harder textures → mixed textures. Having actual food samples at each level allows visual and tactile exploration before eating. The child can see and touch the texture they'll work on. This reduces anxiety and surprise. Commercially available or home-prepared graded texture samples become the therapy 'ladder' — each step slightly more challenging than the last, with the child moving up only when ready.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Create a texture progression for a specific food the child is working on. Example with banana: smooth puree → puree with tiny soft pieces → mashed with visible chunks → soft banana pieces → whole banana slices. Create visual 'ladder' showing steps. Let child see and touch each level. Work on tolerance at current level before moving up. Other examples: yogurt (smooth → with small fruit pieces → with larger pieces → with crunchy toppings); pasta (very soft, overcooked → normal → with small sauce pieces → with chunky sauce). Document what level child is at for each food category.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Respect choking hazard guidelines for child's age. Monitor for actual difficulty chewing/swallowing vs. sensory aversion. Work with feeding therapist for significant difficulties.

item.name: Food Interaction Tools (Picks, Tweezers, Mashers)
item.description: Direct hand contact with challenging textures can trigger aversion before food even reaches the mouth. Food interaction tools create distance between the child and the texture while still allowing exploration. Food picks and toothpicks let children poke, explore, and eventually spear foods without full hand contact. Tweezers and tongs allow picking up and moving foods. Child-sized mashers let them create textures themselves (mashing = control). This indirect interaction reduces sensory overwhelm while keeping the child engaged with food. It also gives them control — they decide when and how to interact. Control reduces anxiety and increases willingness to explore.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Provide food picks, toothpicks, small tongs or tweezers, child-sized forks, and mashers at meals. Let child use tools to interact with foods they're not yet eating. Encourage exploration: 'Can you poke it? What does it feel like? Can you squish it?' The progression: look at food → touch with tool → touch with finger → touch to lips → tiny taste → small bite. Tools help with early steps. Some children enjoy mashing or cutting foods themselves — this gives control over texture. Don't require eating; just interaction.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Use age-appropriate tools without sharp points. Supervise use. Focus on exploration, not forcing eating.

item.name: Sensory Play Materials for Non-Food Texture Exposure
item.description: Tactile sensitivity often extends beyond food to general touch. Children who are tactilely defensive may benefit from broader sensory play that builds overall texture tolerance. When the hands become more comfortable with varied textures through play, the mouth often follows. Sensory play materials — play dough, kinetic sand, water beads, slime, sensory bins with varied textures — build general tactile tolerance. The play context removes pressure and makes texture exploration fun. While not directly about food, this 'texture play' helps the nervous system become more comfortable with varied sensory input overall, which transfers to oral texture tolerance.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Provide regular opportunities for sensory play with varied textures. Options: play dough (smooth, then add texture with beads or rice); kinetic sand; cooked pasta for squishing; water beads; slime (varying thickness); sensory bins with dry materials (rice, beans, sand). Let child control their level of engagement — some children need to start with tools before using hands. Gradually increase texture variety and 'messy' quality. Connect to food when appropriate: 'This feels squishy like mashed potatoes.' Regular sensory play reduces overall tactile defensiveness.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Supervise for mouthing of non-food items, especially with young children. Use non-toxic materials. Be aware of choking hazards with small items.

item.name: Food Chaining Reference Cards
item.description: Food chaining is a therapeutic technique that expands food acceptance by making small, systematic changes from accepted foods. Each 'chain' moves from a food the child already eats to a slightly different version, eventually reaching a target food. Reference cards provide visual guides for creating chains: if the child eats X, here are logical next steps toward Y. For texture aversions specifically, chains move through texture progressions while keeping other properties (taste, color, shape, brand) consistent. The small changes are less threatening than introducing completely new foods. Food chaining reference cards help parents and therapists identify the next logical step in a child's texture progression.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Create food chains starting from the child's currently accepted foods. For texture progression: Current: smooth applesauce → slightly textured applesauce → applesauce with tiny soft pieces → diced soft apples → apple slices. Change only ONE property at a time (texture OR taste OR appearance OR brand, not multiple). Document chains and track progress. Commercial food chaining cards are available (SOS Approach to Feeding, Melanie Potock resources). Work with feeding therapist to create individualized chains. The goal is success at each step — if a step fails, make it smaller.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Move at child's pace. Never force. If a step causes significant distress, the step is too big — break it down further.

item.name: Visual Texture Expectation Cards
item.description: Unexpected textures often trigger more severe reactions than anticipated ones. A child who could tolerate a texture if they expected it may gag if surprised by it. Visual texture expectation cards show what a food looks and feels like before eating, reducing surprise and anxiety. Cards can show: what the food looks like, what texture category it belongs to (smooth, lumpy, chewy, crunchy, mixed), and what it will feel like in the mouth. This 'texture preview' prepares the nervous system for what's coming. Some cards include the child's personal rating of the texture, helping them build self-awareness about their own responses. Predictability reduces defensive reactions.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Create texture category cards with actual photos: smooth (yogurt, pudding, mashed potatoes), lumpy (oatmeal with pieces, chunky soup), chewy (bread, meat, gummy candy), crunchy (crackers, chips, raw carrots), mixed (sandwich, casserole, salad). Before meals, preview what textures will be on the plate. Let child see and categorize. For new foods, create a card showing what to expect. Include child's personal ratings ('I can eat this,' 'I'm working on this,' 'Too hard right now'). This builds self-awareness and communication about texture responses.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Use cards to inform and reduce anxiety, not to force eating. Respect child's current limits while working on expansion.

item.name: Oral Motor Exercise Tools and Games
item.description: Oral motor skills — tongue movement, chewing patterns, bolus formation, and swallowing coordination — affect texture tolerance. If a child can't efficiently chew and move a texture in their mouth, that texture feels more threatening. Oral motor exercise tools strengthen and coordinate the muscles used in eating. Blow toys (whistles, bubbles, blow games) strengthen lip and cheek muscles. Straw drinking with varied thicknesses builds suction and oral control. Chewing exercises build jaw strength. When oral motor skills improve, more textures become manageable. The child isn't just tolerating the sensory experience — they're also physically capable of managing the food. Both components matter for texture expansion.

additionalProperty.name: DIY Option
additionalProperty.value: Incorporate oral motor exercises into daily routine. Blow toys: whistles, party blowers, bubbles, cotton ball 'soccer' (blow cotton ball across table). Straw activities: thick smoothie through straw, blowing through straw into water, straw painting. Chewing: chewy tubes, resistive food (bagels, dried fruit) if tolerated. Tongue exercises: licking peanut butter or preferred food off lips, tongue click games, moving food around mouth intentionally. Work with speech-language pathologist or OT for specific exercises matched to child's oral motor needs. Regular practice (few minutes daily) builds skills over time.

additionalProperty.name: Safety Note
additionalProperty.value: Use age-appropriate tools. Supervise blow toys and small items. Ensure any chewing items are safe and appropriate for child's oral motor level.

socialMedia.caption: When they gag before they even taste it. Some children's mouths process textures as overwhelming. Lumps, chunks, and 'bits' trigger gagging and refusal. Here are 9 materials that help build texture tolerance: oral sensory tools, vibrating oral tools, flavor dips, graded texture samples, food interaction tools, sensory play materials, food chaining cards, texture expectation cards, and oral motor exercise tools. Build texture tolerance step by step. 🔖 #FoodTextureAversion #SensoryFeeding #PinnacleBlooms
